meta.title: Banxico — unanimous June 2026 rate vote?
meta.question: Will the Banxico Governing Board's June 25, 2026, interest rate decision be unanimous (5-0 vote)?
meta.resolution_criteria: Resolves YES if and only if the Bank of Mexico (Banxico) Governing Board's monetary policy decision announced on June 25, 2026 is a unanimous 5-0 vote (all five members voting the same way). Any split (e.g. 4-1, 3-2) resolves NO.
meta.background: On May 7, 2026, the Bank of Mexico (Banxico) Governing Board cut its benchmark interest rate to 6.50% in a 3-2 split vote. Board members Jonathan Heath and Irene Espinosa Cantellano dissented, preferring to hold the rate at 6.75%. The central bank cited a contraction in economic activity and revised its Q2 2026 inflation forecast upward to 4.1%, while targeting convergence to its 3% goal by Q2 2027. In its statement the board signaled that its roughly two-year easing cycle had come to an end and that it would take a more cautious, data-dependent stance going forward. The five voting members are Governor Victoria Rodriguez Ceja and Deputy Governors Galia Borja Gomez, Omar Mejia Torres, Jonathan Heath, and Irene Espinosa Cantellano. The next scheduled monetary policy decision is June 25, 2026.
meta.background_note: As-of (2026-05-14) background reconstructed from the public, sourced facts of the May 7 2026 Banxico decision; injected as sealed-replay evidence.
meta.causal_thesis: The May 7 split vote (3-2) revealed two persistent dissenters (Heath, Espinosa) who prefer holding rates. The board's signal that the easing cycle ended and a cautious stance begins may reduce internal disagreement if all members align on data dependence. However, the dissenters' prior preference for no cut could persist if they view the new stance as too dovish or if economic conditions diverge, leading to another split.
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.25 (counted n=1); simulation-conditional 0.0 (resolved mass 0.06); headline 0.25 via combiner_unavailable_range_only. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
unresolved [unresolved_unknown_state]: 0.556 — explicit other_unknown_state mass; feasible-action bounds computed, never assigned prior/0.5
unresolved [unresolved_future_decision]: 0.388 — advance simulated time to the deadline; the obligation reopens the decision

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the simulation-conditional forecast; the grounded prior is reported separately; this is a stated reason Lean V2 must not become the default yet

procedure.rule: Board discusses economic conditions and rate proposal.
Each member votes for one of the options: cut, hold, or hike.

resolution.interpretation: The Banxico Governing Board's June 25, 2026 monetary policy decision is a unanimous 5-0 vote.
resolution.yes_means: All five members vote the same way (unanimous).
resolution.no_means: At least one member dissents (split vote).

shared_conditions.claim: Mexico's economy is in a contraction with elevated inflation, creating a cautious monetary policy environment.
The board signaled an end to the easing cycle and a cautious data-dependent stance, increasing pressure for consensus.
Governor Victoria Rodriguez Ceja likely leads toward consensus, but recent split shows dissent is possible.
The board has a known split: Heath and Espinosa dissented on May 7, 2026, indicating a persistent hawkish faction.
All members have access to the same economic data and forecasts, including upward inflation revision and contraction.

provenance.definition: All five voting members of the Banxico Governing Board vote the same way on the interest rate decision.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any Banxico monetary policy decision where the vote is recorded as 5-0.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Any decision with a split vote (e.g., 4-1, 3-2) or where a member is absent/recused.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: On May 7, 2026, the vote was 3-2, not unanimous.
cases.basis_quote: the Bank of Mexico (Banxico) Governing Board cut its benchmark interest rate to 6.50% in a 3-2 split vote.

hypotheses.claim: Victoria Rodriguez Ceja privately believes that restoring unanimous decisions is critical for market credibility after the split vote, and she will use her leadership to broker a compromise, possibly by shifting her own vote to match the majority or by persuading dissenters with a moderate rate path
beliefs: Unanimity signals stability and reduces market uncertainty
The split vote damaged the board's reputation
goals: Achieve a unanimous vote on June 25
commitments: Publicly stated commitment to data-dependent stance
hypotheses.pressures: Strong internal pressure to heal the split and present a united front
hypotheses.action_if_state: Actively negotiate with Heath and Espinosa, possibly offering a symbolic hold or a small cut to secure their votes

hypotheses.claim: Victoria Rodriguez Ceja privately accepts that a split is inevitable given the deep disagreement on inflation outlook, and she will not force unanimity, prioritizing policy correctness over optics.
beliefs: Forcing unanimity could lead to a bad policy decision
The board's diversity of views is healthy
hypotheses.pressures: Less pressure from markets after initial split absorbed
hypotheses.action_if_state: Allow a free vote and accept the outcome, even if split

hypotheses.claim: Galia Borja Gomez privately prioritizes board cohesion and will align with the Governor's push for unanimity, possibly adjusting her vote if needed to secure a consensus.
beliefs: Board unity is essential for policy transmission
The Governor's leadership should be supported
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote with the Governor's preferred stance, even if it means changing her own preference

hypotheses.claim: Galia Borja Gomez privately holds a strong view on the appropriate rate (e.g., cut further) and will not compromise, even if it means a split vote.
beliefs: Economic contraction warrants more easing
Inflation is transitory
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote for a cut regardless of others' positions

hypotheses.claim: Omar Mejia Torres privately sees himself as a swing vote and is open to either side, willing to join a unanimous decision if a middle ground exists, such as a hold.
beliefs: The economy is in a delicate balance
Compromise is possible on a hold
hypotheses.action_if_state: Support a hold or a small cut that can attract both hawks and doves

hypotheses.claim: Omar Mejia Torres privately believes that further cuts are necessary to support growth and will vote for a cut even if it causes a split.
beliefs: Inflation is under control in the medium term
Growth risks dominate
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote for a cut, accepting a split

hypotheses.claim: Jonathan Heath privately remains convinced that rates should be held or even raised due to inflation risks, and he will dissent again if the majority votes to cut.
beliefs: Inflation is sticky and above target
Premature easing would be a mistake
commitments: Dissented in May for a hold
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote to hold or raise, dissenting if necessary

hypotheses.claim: Jonathan Heath privately is willing to accept a hold as a compromise, especially if the majority moves to hold rather than cut, allowing a unanimous decision.
beliefs: A hold is the safest middle ground
Unanimity has value
commitments: Dissented for a hold in May
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote for a hold, joining a unanimous decision

hypotheses.claim: Irene Espinosa Cantellano privately holds a strong hawkish view, believing that inflation expectations are unanchored, and she will dissent again if the board cuts rates.
beliefs: Inflation is the primary threat
Credibility requires holding rates
commitments: Dissented in May for a hold
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote to hold or raise, dissenting if majority cuts

hypotheses.claim: Irene Espinosa Cantellano privately coordinates with Jonathan Heath and will vote the same way as him, whether that is a hold or a dissent, valuing their alliance.
beliefs: Heath's analysis is sound
Strength in numbers
hypotheses.action_if_state: Vote identically to Heath, whether hold or dissent
